items.myth: A commit stores a diff from the previous commit.
items.reality: A commit stores a *full snapshot* (tree of file blobs). Git is so efficient because identical blobs are deduplicated by content hash and packed together — diffs are *computed on display*, not stored.
items.example:
```
git cat-file -p HEAD     # shows tree, parent, author, message — not a diff
```

items.myth: A branch is a copy of the repository.
items.reality: A branch is a single tiny file (`.git/refs/heads/<name>`) holding a commit SHA. Creating one is O(1) regardless of repo size — no copy, no clone.
`git add` updates the *index* with a hash of the current file contents. The file stays where it is. If you edit again, you must `add` again to stage the new version.
items.example:
```bash
git add file.py
vim file.py        # NEW changes are NOT staged yet
git diff           # shows what's still unstaged
```

items.myth: Deleting a branch deletes the commits on it.
items.reality: Commits stay in the object database; they're just no longer pointed at. If they're not reachable from any branch/tag/reflog they'll eventually be garbage-collected — but reachable ones (via reflog) can be recovered for ~90 days by default.
items.example:
```bash
git reflog                    # shows recent HEAD positions
git switch -c saved-work <sha>  # recover commits from a deleted branch
```
